terms.definition: Testing style where you state an invariant a function must always satisfy and a generator searches the input space for counterexamples.
The de-facto Python property-based testing library; provides strategies, shrinking and stateful machines on top of pytest.
Hypothesis abstraction describing a space of values to generate (e.g. `st.integers()`, `st.lists(st.text())`); composable into richer distributions.
Hypothesis's automatic process of minimising a failing input to the simplest form that still falsifies the property — what makes PBT bug reports debuggable.
A statement of the form 'for all inputs X, condition Y holds'; the unit of assertion in PBT.
The specific input Hypothesis found (after shrinking) that violates the stated property; reported as a one-line reproducer.
PBT variant where Hypothesis generates random sequences of operations on a stateful object and checks invariants after each step (`RuleBasedStateMachine`).
A relationship between outputs for related inputs (e.g. 'permuting the dataset doesn't change the rank order'), used when no single oracle exists.
Invariant of the form `decode(encode(x)) == x` (or `parse(serialize(x)) == x`); one of the most useful and easiest to state PBT properties.
`f(f(x)) == f(x)` — applying the function twice has the same effect as once; common for normalisers and migrations.
